data.title: What to do in Hong Kong this Christmas: Michelin-star feasts, circus acts and charity events
data.description:
As the year draws to a close, Hong Kong is awash with set menus, cultural happenings, themed brunches and extravagant countdown parties. The offerings run the gamut from high tradition (yes, more turkey) to the decidedly unconventional (fried chicken, anyone?).
Tai Kwun has launched its “Simple Gifts of Joy” celebration, running until January 4. The main event is “Circus Plays”, a programme bringing in high-calibre international circus acts, including the Asia premiere of The Genesis from Denmark and the Hong Kong premiere of Spiral Slide from the United States.

Focusing on the philanthropic, Upper House has an art-led tree in collaboration with artist Cj Hendry and auction house Phillips. It’s composed of 200 oversized plush green “juju” toys, each “adoptable” for HK$688, with all proceeds benefiting the charity Mother’s Choice.
If you’re suffering from turkey fatigue, May Chow’s Little Bao is throwing the rule book out the kitchen window with its Sichuan-style fried chicken served with a crème fraîche and ikura dip. It headlines an eight-course festive set that also includes a 24-hour sous-vide short rib bao and a rich bowl of chicken innard noodles.

For those who demand classic luxury, the city’s fine-dining stalwarts are obliging. At The Landmark Mandarin Oriental, the three-Michelin-starred Amber has eight-course festive dinners on the cards, while the Michelin-starred Beefbar is offering six-course menus for Christmas and New Year’s Eve.
Those with more contemporary tastes might venture out to Mosu Hong Kong for a festive lunch or dinner featuring a minimalist Korean degustation, or to Kanesaka, which has a Christmas omakase dinner bringing in the best catch from Japanese waters.

For something a notch below the multi-Michelin-star stratosphere, the all-day brasseries are your best bet. Upper House’s Salisterra is covering all bases with festive set lunches, dinners and a special Christmas Day menu. Just a hop and a skip away, The Continental is offering four-course dinners on Christmas Eve, Boxing Day and New Year’s Eve.
Whatever your inclination this December, it’s clear there is no shortage of ways to celebrate the festive season this year.
Festive spreads, delivered
Prefer to celebrate at home? The city’s holiday takeaway menus have you sorted.
Petrus

At Island Shangri-La’s Petrus, chef Uwe Opocensky’s HK$8,888 holiday meal is built around his golden roast butterball turkey with traditional trimmings. For smaller celebrations, the premium roast turkey gift box (HK$2,288) offers all the trimmings of a five-star feast. shangri-la.com
Heimat

Heimat leans into continental comfort this year, with chef Peter Find’s German-style takeaway featuring roast goose (HK$1,280) and duck (HK$980) with potato dumplings, plus add-ons (from HK$80) such as sausage platters and vegetable sides. heimat.hk
Market Place

Market Place’s 12-person Christmas Set (HK$2,528) goes generous with roasted chicken, slow-cooked oxtail, lobster and roasted vegetables. The six-person set (HK$1,199) keeps things cosy with lamb rack, pork knuckle and seasonal sides. marketplacehk.com
Butterfly Patisserie

For sweets, Rosewood Hong Kong’s Butterfly Patisserie offers a series of seasonal creations by executive pastry chef Jonathan Soukdeo, including a Mont Blanc Christmas log cake (HK$588) and ornament-inspired pastries and matcha chiffon rolls (HK$98). rosewoodhotels.com

data.title: Huawei bolsters AI ambitions with dedicated foundation model unit, recruits global talent
data.description:
Huawei Technologies is doubling down on foundation models with a newly formed unit, heating up the race for dominance among Chinese tech giants in the artificial intelligence sector.
The AI foundation model unit was established under Huawei’s 2012 Laboratories, the company’s strategic research and development arm that covered domains ranging from wireless technology to AI, according to a report by Chinese media outlet China Star Markets this week.
The department was actively recruiting AI talent, the report said. Huawei confirmed the establishment of the foundation model department without providing further details.
In a recruitment ad posted in late October, the Shenzhen-based telecommunications gear giant said it was building a world-class AI team by wooing global talent.
Richard Yu Chengdong, Huawei’s consumer group head, reposted the recruitment ad on social media and urged young talent to join Huawei to “make the world’s most powerful AI”.

Yu was recently made chairman of the Investment Review Board at Huawei, which put him in charge of the company’s AI strategies.
Huawei’s latest move comes as China’s Big Tech firms and start-ups are locked in cutthroat competition to develop AI models that rival those of Western counterparts such as OpenAI, while also searching for ways to commercialise their technologies.
Last month, Huawei appointed Zhou Yuefeng, formerly head of the data storage product line, as CEO of Huawei’s cloud computing business group. Zhang Pingan, the former CEO, was named chairman of the cloud unit.
It followed a major restructuring of the company’s cloud computing unit in August, the Post reported. The subsidiary – responsible for AI model development, cloud services and data centre solutions – merged several key departments to prioritise AI-related businesses.
The US-sanctioned company is doubling down on its efforts to provide AI hardware and software services in response to soaring demand, as it continues to navigate Washington’s restrictions on China’s access to advanced chips, particularly those from Nvidia. Huawei is building its home-grown alternatives to Nvidia’s much-coveted AI processors.
Ren Zhengfei, Huawei founder and CEO, said the company would focus on AI applications that could solve current problems across industries.
“Our company’s research will focus on the next three to five years, especially on the application of AI models, big data and massive computing power in the industry, agriculture and technology sectors,” Ren said in a speech last month.

data.title: China ‘Drink Can Queen’ Guan Yuxiang grows US$1.4 billion business empire after retirement
data.description:
Chinese entrepreneur Guan Yuxiang built her drink can empire from scratch after retirement, earning her the titles of the country’s “Drink Can Queen” and “the greatest retired female worker.”
Born in Shenyang, in northeastern China’s Liaoning province in 1939, Guan joined a factory in Beijing at the age of 19. Like many in her generation, she dedicated three decades of her life to the same company before retiring at 50.
However, unlike many who embrace a restful retirement, Guan chose to embark on a new business venture.

She initially established a small company to produce instruments and metres for measuring various physical quantities, but this endeavour met with failure.
Believing that failures are stepping stones to success, Guan did not give up; instead, she began to study the market closely.
In 1994, while travelling in the southern island province of Hainan, Guan observed that the region was home to numerous soft drink factories.
She noted that the packaging materials they used were primarily sourced from Taiwan and South Korea.
With a determined spirit, Guan managed to raise 300,000 yuan (US$42,000) and founded O.R.G. Packaging Co. Ltd. (ORG) in Hainan.

The company procured its drink can manufacturing equipment from Taiwan. The seller, however, initially refused to provide the equipment’s technical information, claiming it would be too complex for them to understand.
Guan later reflected that this experience underscored the importance of technology.
In 1995, when the energy drink company Red Bull entered China, Guan and her team reportedly made 41 visits to the company before successfully persuading them to use their services.
Red Bull agreed to allow ORG to produce their cans on the condition that they implement a powder coating technique, which was an international standard yet unfamiliar to Chinese drink can manufacturers at the time.

Guan mortgaged the factory to import this powder coating technology from Singapore.
One successful order opened the door to many others, and ORG became the can supplier for major brands such as Tsingtao Brewery, Want Want, and Jia Duo Bao.
ORG’s partnership with Red Bull has flourished for three decades.
Guan has persistently guided the company in researching and developing new technologies, resulting in over 100 patents owned by the firm.
Over the course of 30 years, the company transformed from a 16-member workshop into a publicly listed enterprise with over 4,000 employees.

Since 2020, the company’s annual sales volume has consistently exceeded 10 billion yuan (US$1.4 billion), solidifying its reputation as an industry leader.
In addition to her business success, Guan is dedicated to public welfare. She established a charity foundation to support impoverished students and has donated more than 20 million yuan.
Guan firmly believes that one should not be constrained by age.
“Ageing is a natural law that no one can escape, but what truly defines a person are their attitude, passion, and virtue,” she asserts.

data.title: In India, tycoon’s call to adopt ‘996’ work culture in China spurs debate
data.description:
India’s parliament is debating a draft law that would give employees the right to ignore work calls, emails and messages outside official hours, weeks after a tycoon’s call for a 72-hour work week sparked a furore on social media.
The Right to Disconnect Bill 2025, introduced on December 5 by MP Supriya Sule, would empower authorities to impose penalties on non-compliant firms.
Supporters argue the proposal recognises workers’ rights in an era of always-on corporate culture. Critics, however, say enforcement would be difficult in a labour-surplus economy with many Indian employees working long hours.
In an interview with Republic TV last month, N. R. Narayana Murthy, founder of leading IT company Infosys, sparked a debate when he urged Indian workers to emulate China’s work ethic to advance their country.
“There is a saying in China, 9, 9, 6. You know what it means? 9am to 9pm, 6 days a week. And that is a 72-hour work week.” He said young Indians should follow the same work routine.
Murthy also made a similar call in a separate TV interview in 2023, saying “the average Indian’s attitude tends to be not so work-driven”.
Online users in India have pushed back against his comments, saying they were regularly tired due to late-night work calls, weekend emails from their companies and pressure to remain contactable all day long.

Under India’s proposed law, employees should be paid overtime if they are asked to work beyond regular hours. It also suggests establishing a process to address work emergencies.
The proposal is welcomed by human resources experts who say it recognises workers’ rights and protects them from overworking, but they concede that enforcing the law would be challenging.
Prabir Jha, founder and CEO of Prabir Jha People Advisory, said that the spirit of the law was “uplifting”. Even if the law would not be implemented, it had created “some meaningful debate and hopefully, corporate introspection”, he added.
There would be practical challenges in validating complaints, imposing penalties and determining urgent and non-urgent work, Jha said. The legal framework’s structure would also be key to deterrence, he added.
Murthy’s call might not be linked to the proposed law, according to Jha. Even if it were approved, implementation would not happen soon, as other related rules had to be looked into, he added.
Aparna Mittal, a corporate lawyer, agreed and said the law’s effectiveness would be contingent on its smooth implementation.

“We already have multiple laws that regulate work hours, but their implementation has been quite lax across sectors,” said Mittal, who is also the founder of anti-discrimination consultancy Samana Centre.
Under the proposed law, working hours must remain capped at 48 hours a week. However, private companies often exploit young employees, who would work long hours with low pay, according to industry observers.
Recent data from the International Labour Organization shows workers in India are among the most overworked globally, with about 51 per cent working at least 49 hours every week.
“While I would like to see corporations promoting healthy work-life balance for all, and respecting personal time post-designated work hours, I am very sceptical about whether management of companies will actively adopt or follow such norms,” Mittal said.
A statement issued in tandem with the draft law says new regulations are needed because digital tools have created a culture of constant availability, leading workers to sleep less and experience more stress.
Amritraj Kaushal, a veteran criminal and corporate lawyer, said the proposed law could enhance the work-life balance of employees, particularly in the private sector, where long hours were the norm.
Many employees experience a constant pressure to remain available
Amritraj Kaushal, criminal and corporate lawyer
“As digital communication continues to blur the line between professional and personal space, many employees experience a constant pressure to remain available, often without compensation or rest,” he said.
The law would compel organisations to set clear expectations for work hours and overtime procedures, Kaushal said.
Evidence from other jurisdictions suggests that companies would improve their labour practices when such laws are in place, according to Kaushal.
Lloyd Mathias, an independent director on corporate boards, said the law would represent a “significant cultural pivot” in India, leading to better employee well-being and overall higher productivity.
Nonetheless, it would be a major challenge to push employers to change their attitudes and place greater emphasis on staff welfare, Mathias said.
“India’s workplaces typically have a deep-rooted hustle culture, with an expectation of constant availability. Changing this ingrained behaviour and the management mindset in a labour surplus market will be a significant hurdle.”

data.title: Hong Kong national security police arrest 9 over military-style combat drills
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested nine men for suspected involvement in military-style combat training, the first such case under the domestic national security law, after raiding a factory space in San Po Kong.
The force’s National Security Department said on Friday that some of the suspects were friends and had allegedly gathered near the scene of the Tai Po fire wearing clothing typically associated with anti-government activists during the 2019 social unrest.
Officers raided the factory space on Thursday night and seized several suspected explosive devices, at least five converted air guns, five military knives and a long sword, police said, adding that a 3D printer was also discovered at the scene.
Six men, aged 20 to 25, were apprehended at the scene, while three others, aged 20 to 22, were arrested subsequently in Tuen Mun and Yau Ma Tei.
The suspects were still being held for questioning, the force said.
The raid follows a nearly two-year investigation after it received tip-offs about the “illegal drilling” or military-style training activity at the factory premises.
Chief Superintendent Steve Li Kwai-wah of the National Security Department warned that such activity showed there were still signs of anti-government activities in the city.
The force said it was investigating the seized weapons and whether the men had connections with “external forces”, as well as why some were allegedly present at the scene of the Tai Po fire.
Under Section 13 of the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance, a person who provides unlawful drilling can face seven years in jail, and those who undergo the drills could also be subject to three years’ imprisonment.
According to the ordinance, such activities include training or drilling in the use of offensive weapons and the practice of military exercises.
It is the first time that police have applied this section to arrest suspects regarding illegal drilling.

data.title: Hong Kong Sevens injected almost US$100 million into local economy on Kai Tak debut
data.description:
This year’s Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens pumped almost US$100 million into the city’s economy, according to a report from leading global analytics firm Nielsen.
Held at Kai Tak Stadium for the first time, the three-day event in March had a total attendance of 110,907, the report said, with Saturday drawing the largest number of fans to the 50,000-seat venue.
While local supporters made up the lion’s share of that figure, almost 24 per cent were visitors, with officials estimating that 10,000 had made the trip.
That was reflected in the US$12.5 million Nielsen said was spent on flights – the largest outlay when it came to direct economic impact – with spending on food and drink coming in second at US$12.1 million.
The action on the pitch reportedly reached 11.7 million viewers overseas and was broadcast across 16 markets, while media from 25 countries and territories covered the 72 matches that took place.

According to Nielsen, the US$97.9 million generated, of which US$43.2 million was considered secondary spending, placed the Sevens between the US$120 million typically brought in by a Formula 1 race and the US$69 million associated with hosting a multi-day world championships for an Olympic sport.
Direct economic impact was placed at US$54.7 million, just marginally higher than the estimated US$54.5 million for the eight-day Hong Kong Football Festival in July.
Of the city’s other major sporting events, racing would figure prominently, but the Hong Kong Jockey Club does not typically provide numbers for individual occasions. However, this week it predicted that visitors would bring in HK$35 million (US$4.5 million) for the Hong Kong International Races.
And in its report for the 2024-25 financial year, the Jockey Club said it had paid HK$28.8 billion in betting duty and profits tax to the Hong Kong government.
LIV Golf, meanwhile, has been estimated to generate between HK$300 million and HK$500 million, but the league does not make those figures public.

data.title: Hong Kong needs to attract global champions to broaden fundraising base: FSDC
data.description:
Hong Kong will need to attract global champion firms across different sectors to raise funds to strengthen the city’s role as an international financial centre over the next 10 years, according to a concept paper issued by the Financial Services Development Council (FSDC) on Friday.
“Global markets do not stand still,” said Benjamin Hung Pi-cheng, chairman of the FSDC, at a media briefing where the council outlined a wide range of ideas to bring the city’s capital market to the next level. “Hong Kong has every opportunity to shape, innovate and lead.”
The FSDC, a government-backed think tank, conducts studies and research to find ways to strengthen the city as an international financial centre.
While Hong Kong’s stock market had been a fundraising hub for mainland Chinese companies for decades, the next step was to develop the city as a platform for companies from Southeast Asia, the Middle East, Europe and America to list or issue bonds and other asset classes, Hung said.

“From new biotechnology companies or fintech companies to global leading companies, we want to attract them to raise funds here by issuing equities, bonds or other asset classes,” Hung said. “Only in Hong Kong can global companies access a very large pool of money, which is not just international capital but also China’s capital.”
Hong Kong could attract more global firms to list by promoting itself more in the Middle East, Southeast Asia and other emerging markets, as well as in Europe and America, said Andrew Weir, vice-chairman of the FSDC.
Rocky Tung Yat-ngok, director and head of policy research at the FSDC, said potential listing candidates could include European luxury brands, artificial intelligence and technology companies from mainland China and the US, as well as commodities and mining companies in the Middle East or Southeast Asia.
“To achieve these goals, Hong Kong would need to continue to improve its listing regime to facilitate such companies, while considering the introduction of market makers to promote more turnover for the relatively smaller listed companies,” Tung said.
While the average daily turnover of the stock market almost doubled this year to HK$255.8 billion (US$32.8 billion) in the first 11 months, trading was mostly concentrated in the largest 100 stocks.
“After global champion companies list in Hong Kong, we need to give them reasons to continue to stay here,” Tung said. “This is why it is important to enhance the turnover across all types of companies.”
The FSDC paper also called for cross-border trading schemes between Hong Kong and mainland China to include a wider range of asset classes. The connect schemes began in 2014 by linking stock markets between Shanghai and Hong Kong, with the Shenzhen leg added two years later.
The schemes, which allow cross-border trading between mainland China and international investors via Hong Kong, have since expanded to bonds, swaps and wealth management.
“We would like to see the connect schemes to include other asset classes, such as commodities and real estate investment trusts, to strengthen Hong Kong’s unique role of connecting international investors with mainland markets,” Hung said.
The FSDC paper also highlighted the importance of promoting more digital products and tokenised asset trading in the coming years.
Hong Kong’s market regulator, the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC), introduced a licensing regime for virtual asset trading platforms in recent years. The SFC had granted licences to 11 operators as of July, while approving only five cryptocurrency tokens for retail trading in the city: bitcoin, ether, Solana, Avax and Link.
“Hong Kong has the digital assets regulatory framework,” Weir said. “The challenge is to be a leading, cutting-edge, global player in the space, but with enough safeguards to mitigate the inherent risk.”

data.title: Philippines’ Vice-President Duterte faces new corruption charges, sparking political storm
data.description:
A group of civilians including two former government officials, filed a corruption complaint against Philippine Vice-President Sara Duterte, in a fresh political challenge for the estranged deputy of President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr.
The complaint, filed with the Ombudsman’s office on Friday, alleged that Duterte and 15 other officials, misused 612.5 million pesos (US$10.4 million) in public funds through “coordinated schemes” implemented across both the Office of the Vice-President and the Department of Education, which she used to concurrently head until mid-2024, according to a media briefer provided by the complainants.
The group claimed Duterte and the other officials committed criminal violations including the anti-corruption law as well as plunder, bribery and malversation.

Duterte’s office did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The complainants include former presidential adviser Teresita Quintos-Deles and former finance undersecretary Cielo Magno.
The allegations echo the accusations in the impeachment complaint filed against Duterte, which was shelved by the Senate in August after the Supreme Court ruled it unconstitutional. The Ombudsman, Jesus Crispin Remulla, can endorse the corruption complaint against Duterte before the Sandiganbayan anti-corruption court.
A conviction will not bar her from running for president in the 2028 elections – where she is a front runner according to polls – unless it becomes final.


The complaint against the vice-president comes as a widening corruption scandal involving billions of dollars in government funds meant for flood mitigation projects grips the Southeast Asian nation. Duterte has said she is confident she will not be implicated in the scandal.
With an appeal by the House of Representatives to revive the impeachment complaint pending with the Supreme Court, the corruption complaint filed with the Office of the Ombudsman – now headed by a Marcos ally – could become the next battleground for Duterte and her political opponents.
Marcos and Duterte partnered to win the 2022 elections but their alliance collapsed due to political differences. Marcos is mandated under Philippine laws to step down in 2028 after a single six-year term.
Remulla in October replaced an appointee of ex-president Rodrigo Duterte, the vice-president’s father who’s detained in the International Criminal Court in The Hague for charges related to the war on drugs during his term.

data.title: Year of the Horse 2026: 5 famous works of equine art through the centuries
data.description:
For thousands of years, horses have been important to humanity in both our greatest periods of innovation – as invaluable tools of transport and agriculture – and our darkest times of war, as smart and sturdy companions for soldiers.
From the prehistoric cave paintings in France’s Lascaux Cave to the monumental equestrian statues of Roman emperors, horses have been symbols of status, conquest and heroism.
In later works of art, the animal plays a more complex role, often employed to convey man’s appreciation – and exploitation – of the natural world.
From paintings to books to plays, here are five notable works featuring horses as we get ready for the Year of the Horse 2026.
1. One Hundred Horses (1728)
The monumental handscroll One Hundred Horses is one of the most famous and significant works of China’s Qing dynasty (1644-1911).
It was painted in 1728 for the Yongzheng emperor (1678-1735) by the Italian Jesuit missionary and court artist Giuseppe Castiglione, known in Chinese as Lang Shi-ning.

At nearly eight metres (26ft) long, the scroll depicts precisely one hundred horses grazing, running or resting in a sprawling landscape.
Castiglione broke away from the traditional Chinese painting style by employing Western techniques of subtle shading and lighting for the horses, which symbolise vitality and abundance.
This harmonious, hybrid style has made One Hundred Horses a timeless masterpiece of cross-cultural artistic exchange.
2. Black Beauty (1887)

The titular horse from Anna Sewell’s 1877 novel is Black Beauty, a black stallion whose life story is told in the first person. This made the book one of the first fictional animal autobiographies.
Sewell’s primary purpose for writing Black Beauty was “to induce kindness, sympathy and an understanding treatment of horses”. By giving the horse a voice and detailing his pain and emotions, the book forced readers to empathise with working animals often viewed merely as tools.
The book is often directly credited with helping abolish the cruel practice of using the bearing rein, a strap that forced horses to hold their heads unnaturally high for fashion.
Sewell’s novel became an influential manifesto for anti-cruelty activists, sparking discussions and reforms that ultimately advanced the broader cause of animal rights worldwide.
3. Napoleon Crossing the Alps (1801–1805)

Marengo, a small, grey Arabian stallion, was Napoleon Bonaparte’s most famous war horse.
Known for his speed and endurance, Marengo carried the emperor in many key military campaigns – including the battles of Austerlitz, Jena-Auerstedt and Wagram – as well as during his 1812 retreat from Moscow before his capture at the 1815 Battle of Waterloo.
Marengo’s greatest significance, however, lies in his immortalisation, particularly in Jacques-Louis David’s series of five oil paintings, Napoleon Crossing the Alps (1801–1805).
The paintings offer an idealised, propagandistic vision of Bonaparte crossing the Great St Bernard Pass, through Europe’s Western Alps, in May 1800. In reality, Napoleon rode a mule to navigate the icy terrain, but the dramatically rearing Marengo became a symbol of Napoleon’s heroism and unstoppable power.
After Napoleon was captured at Waterloo, Marengo lived in England until his death in 1831, aged 38. His skeleton is currently displayed at the National Army Museum in London.
Marengo was also a shade of grey that became associated with an overcoat that Bonaparte briefly brought into vogue.
4. War Horse (1982)

Joey, the horse from the 1982 novel War Horse by Michael Morpurgo, symbolises innocent victims and universal suffering during World War I.
Like Black Beauty, War Horse is narrated through the horse’s eyes. Joey witnesses the horrors of the trenches, and his interactions highlight the bonds he forms with both British and German soldiers.
Before Steven Spielberg adapted the book into his 2011 movie of the same name, the UK’s National Theatre made a play out of it in 2007.
This became a stage phenomenon, in part because of the life-size horse puppets it featured. These groundbreaking props, operated on stage by three puppeteers working in tandem, helped the play earn special recognition at the 2011 Tony Awards.
The play even had a successful run at The Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts in 2019.
5. Equus (1973)

Peter Shaffer’s 1973 psychological play, Equus, tells the story of a troubled teenager who blinds six horses. Shaffer was inspired to write Equus after hearing of a crime involving a 17-year-old boy who blinded 26 horses in a northern English town.
The play unfolds during Alan’s psychiatric sessions, which probe the boy’s complex, quasi-religious, erotic fixation on horses. He worships these creatures as godlike figures, and Alan’s religious adoration becomes disorientingly intertwined with sexual attraction.
The original stage production ran at the National Theatre in London from 1973 to 1975. However, the play gained massive renewed attention in 2007 when Harry Potter actor Daniel Radcliffe played the lead role for his West End and Broadway debut.
The play’s controversy was widely publicised due to the then 17-year-old actor’s stage nudity and portrayal of the psychosexual subject matter.
Hong Kong pop idol Hins Cheung King-hin also took on this challenging role in 2014, abandoning his boy-next-door charm for something much darker.

data.title: Flood expert Xu Chongyu leaves Europe for China as climate change speeds up
data.description:
For three decades, Xu Chongyu was Europe’s secret weapon against the rising tides. From his quiet office at the University of Oslo, his hydrological models have guarded cities from Copenhagen to Brussels, predicting floods before the first clouds formed.
But the climate clock was ticking fast and his homeland needed him, so the Chinese-born scientist decided to leave.
A homecoming ceremony was held for Xu on December 3 at North China University of Water Resources and Electric Power (NCWU) in Zhengzhou – a city that still bears the scars of a deadly flood in 2021.
The university’s president, Liu Junguo, declared Xu the institution’s first top-tier overseas recruit. The prominent hydrologist, a tenured professor in Norway and a member of the Norwegian Academy of Science and Letters, had walked away from it all.
“Currently, China’s water conservancy sector is facing severe challenges under global changes. My decision to return to China and join this university is not only a career choice but also a new beginning, allowing me to contribute my lifelong learning, research and insights to my homeland,” Xu said at the event.
NCWU is a higher education institution jointly established by the Ministry of Water Resources and Henan province. It is focused on training professionals in hydraulic engineering.
“Xu is a world-renowned authority in hydrology, having made systematic and pioneering contributions to the key field of hydrological changes driven by global change. He is the first top-tier overseas talent to be recruited by our university full time,” Liu said.
Xu confirmed to the South China Morning Post that he had officially resigned from his position as tenured professor at the University of Oslo and had joined NCWU on a full-time basis.
“I no longer have a working relationship with the University of Oslo; however, in recognition of my over 20 years of service and contributions to the university, they will retain the title of ‘honorary professor’ for me,” he said.
“NCWU … has always been one of the ‘three major water conservancy colleges’ in China, possessing a profound academic foundation and distinct characteristics in the fields of water conservancy, water resources and the environment, which aligns closely with my personal research direction,” he said, explaining why he chose the Chinese institution for the next step in his career.


Xu was born in Xuzhou, Jiangsu province. In 1978, he gained admission to Nanjing University with the highest college entrance examination score in his county. He majored in hydrology and water resources engineering.
After graduating in 1982, he began his career as a hydraulic engineer at the Ministry of Water Resources. In 1986, Xu was recommended for a master’s degree programme at the Vrije Universiteit Brussel in Belgium. He went on to earn a PhD from the institution.
In 1993, Xu was awarded the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) Research Award for Young Scientists, becoming the first Chinese scientist to receive this honour.
After obtaining his doctorate, Xu conducted research and taught at the Vrije Universiteit Brussel and Sweden’s Uppsala University.
Since April 2005, he has been a professor of hydrology in the department of geosciences at the University of Oslo.
Xu has long been engaged in research on global environmental change and hydrological responses, hydrological simulation in different climate zones, water resource management and flood forecasting.
The water balance models he has developed have been applied in several countries, serving as practical tools for water resource assessment under climate change scenarios in Denmark, northern Belgium and central Sweden.
His recent research focuses on the hydrological characteristics of major rivers in China, including those on the Tibetan plateau, as well as on optimising the operation of large-scale reservoirs.
Over the past two decades, he has collaborated with institutions including Wuhan University, Nanjing University, Hohai University, the Institute of Geographic Sciences and Natural Resources Research of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, the Nanjing Institute of Geography and Limnology and the Institute of Tibetan Plateau Research.
In 2016, he was elected a member of the Norwegian Academy of Science and Letters, the highest academic body in Norway. In 2018, he became a member of the Norwegian Academy of Technological Sciences. In 2022, he was awarded the Nordic Association for Hydrology Lifetime Achievement Award in recognition of his contributions.
Xu has published over 600 peer-reviewed papers, with cumulative citations exceeding 30,000.
“Returning to my home country for development has always been an important consideration in my academic career planning,” he said.
“In recent years, the significant strategic needs in areas such as water resource management, climate change response, and ecological environment protection in our country have provided me with opportunities to contribute to national development.”
